section.title: Charge It to My Account
section.sectionRationale: Here Paul makes his boldest move. He asks Philemon to receive Onesimus as he would receive Paul himself, then offers to pay any debt Onesimus owes. In this offer the letter gives us one of Scripture's clearest living pictures of substitution and imputation.
section.thesis: Paul pledges to absorb Onesimus's debt and asks that the runaway be welcomed as Paul himself would be, enacting in miniature the gospel of substitution, receive him as me, and charge his debt to my account.
section.frameNote: One movement in four beats: receive him as me; charge his debt to me; refresh my heart; and the confident hope of a coming visit.

units.label: Receive him as me, charge his debt to me

verses.text: So if you consider me a {{koinonos|partner}}, receive him as you would receive me.
But if he has wronged you in any way or owes you anything, {{ellogeo|charge it to my account}}.
I, Paul, write this with my own hand. I will {{apotino|repay}} it; not to mention that you owe me your very self.
Yes, brother, let me have some {{onaimen|benefit}} from you in the Lord. {{anapauo|Refresh}} my {{splagchna|heart}} in Christ.
Confident of your obedience, I write to you, knowing that you will do even more than I ask.
In the meantime, prepare a guest room for me, because I hope that through your prayers I will be restored to you.

koinonos.gloss: A partner, sharer, one who holds things in common, the personal cousin of the word koinonia from verse 6. Paul appeals to their shared partnership in the gospel: if Philemon counts Paul a partner, then he must welcome Onesimus with the same welcome he would give Paul. The bond of partnership becomes the ground of the request.

ellogeo.gloss: A commercial term meaning to charge to someone's account, to reckon or impute a debt. Paul tells Philemon to put whatever Onesimus owes onto Paul's ledger. The same accounting language describes how our sin is charged to Christ and His righteousness credited to us, making this a vivid picture of imputation.

apotino.gloss: To pay back in full, to repay or make compensation, a legal term for satisfying a debt or damages. Paul writes his personal IOU: I will repay it. He takes on himself a debt he did not incur, standing in Onesimus's place, a small enactment of substitution.

onaimen.gloss: May I have benefit, profit, or joy, an optative form expressing a wish. It is a deliberate play on the name Onesimus, which comes from the same root and means useful or beneficial: Paul is saying, in effect, let me have some Onesimus from you, let me profit from you in the Lord. The pun ties his personal request to the very man he is pleading for.

anapauo.gloss: To refresh, give rest, or revive. Paul now asks for himself the very thing he praised Philemon for doing to others in verse 7: refresh my heart. The request turns Philemon's known kindness back toward Paul, and toward Onesimus, whose welcome would be that refreshment.

splagchna.gloss: The inward parts, used figuratively for the deepest affections, the heart. It closes the thread begun in verse 7 and continued in verse 12: Philemon refreshed the hearts of the saints, Onesimus is Paul's own heart, and now Paul asks Philemon to refresh his heart by receiving him.

basic.context: Paul now makes his boldest request. He asks Philemon to welcome Onesimus exactly as he would welcome Paul himself. Then he goes further: whatever Onesimus owes, whatever wrong he did, Paul says, put it on my account, I will pay it. He signs this promise in his own hand. He asks Philemon to refresh his heart by doing this, says he is sure Philemon will do even more than asked, and tells him to get a guest room ready, because Paul hopes to visit soon.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the emotional and theological peak of the letter. Paul does not just ask for mercy; he offers to take the debt himself and to have Onesimus received in his own place. Whether he means it as a formal promise or a gesture of love, it perfectly pictures the gospel, and it shows that real reconciliation often costs the peacemaker something.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul appeals to their partnership, koinonos, receive him as you would me. He uses the accounting word ellogeo, charge it to my account, and the legal word apotino, I will repay. In verse 20 he puns again on Onesimus with onaimen, let me have benefit from you, and asks Philemon to anapauo, refresh, his splagchna, his heart. The words of business and law are pressed into the service of grace.
basic.typology: Charge it to my account and receive him as you would me are a living picture of the gospel. Our debt of sin is charged to Christ, and He is received in our place; His welcome becomes our welcome. Paul, standing between the offended master and the guilty slave, taking the debt on himself, images the Mediator who reconciles us to God at His own cost.
basic.christ: This is the clearest gospel picture in the letter. Just as Paul says receive Onesimus as me and charge his debt to me, so Christ takes our debt to Himself and gives us His own standing, so that the Father receives us as He receives His Son. Paul's substitution is a shadow; Christ's is the reality, paid not with an IOU but with His blood.
basic.god: God is the great reconciler who bears the cost of restoring the guilty. In Paul's willingness to pay another's debt we glimpse the heart of God, who does not merely overlook wrong but absorbs it, so that the estranged can be welcomed home.
basic.crossReferences: Second Corinthians 5:21 says Christ was made sin for us that we might become God's righteousness. Colossians 2:14 speaks of the record of debt nailed to the cross. Romans 5:8 shows God's love in Christ dying for us while we were still sinners.
basic.oneStory: The whole Bible moves toward a Mediator who pays what we owe and secures our welcome with the Father. Paul's charge it to my account is one small, bright reflection of that gospel, showing that reconciliation is real, costly, and finally grounded in Christ who took our place.

context.literary: The climax of the appeal and its most personal moment. Paul drops into first person emphasis, I, Paul, write this with my own hand, giving the promise the force of a legal note. He renews the earlier language of partnership, benefit, and refreshing, gathering the letter's threads, then softens the pressure with confidence in Philemon and the warm expectation of a visit.
context.historical: Signing in his own hand marks this as a binding personal guarantee, an ancient promissory note. The reminder that Philemon owes Paul his very self likely means Paul had led him to faith. The request to prepare a guest room signals Paul's hoped-for release and the accountability of a coming visit, a gentle encouragement to Philemon to do right.
context.canonical: The passage has long been treasured as an illustration of imputation and substitutionary atonement. The accounting and legal vocabulary, charge it to my account, I will repay, receive him as me, gives concrete, everyday shape to truths the New Testament states directly elsewhere.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To secure Onesimus's full welcome by placing himself in Onesimus's stead, offering to pay any debt and asking that the runaway be received as Paul himself, while expressing confident love that expects Philemon to do even more than requested.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul describes a specific, personal act of debt-bearing love; he is not laying down a general rule of finance. The enduring pattern is that gospel reconciliation is costly and often requires a mediator willing to absorb the wrong, supremely fulfilled in Christ.

debates.question: Is Paul's offer to pay the debt a literal legal promise or a rhetorical gesture of love?

views.label: A genuine, binding pledge
views.case: Paul signs it in his own hand with the formal language of a promissory note, I will repay it, treating the debt as real and assumable and giving Philemon no financial reason to withhold welcome.

views.label: A loving gesture that also reminds Philemon of his own debt
views.case: In the same breath Paul notes that Philemon owes him his very self, gently implying the account is more than settled, so the offer functions as much to melt resistance as to transfer funds.

debates.bearing: Either way the effect is the same and the gospel picture stands: Paul places himself between the offended and the offender and takes the cost upon himself, so that Onesimus can be freely received.

study.originalLanguages: The vocabulary of the marketplace and the courtroom is bent toward grace. Paul appeals as a koinonos, a partner: receive him as you would me. He uses ellogeo, an accounting term, charge it to my account, and apotino, a legal term, I will repay it, signing the note in his own hand. Then he puns once more on the name Onesimus with onaimen, may I have benefit from you, and asks Philemon to anapauo, refresh, his splagchna, his heart, closing the thread from verse 7. Business language becomes the language of substitution.

typology.type: Receive him as you would receive me, verse 17
typology.fulfillment: The Father receives believers as He receives His Son; united to Christ, we are welcomed in His name and with His standing, Ephesians 1:6.

typology.type: Charge his debt to my account, I will repay it, verses 18 to 19
typology.fulfillment: Christ takes our debt of sin upon Himself and cancels the record against us, paying in full at the cross, Colossians 2:14 and 2 Corinthians 5:21.

study.christ: This is the letter's brightest window onto the gospel. As Paul says of Onesimus, receive him as me and charge his debt to me, so Christ takes our debt to Himself and clothes us in His own welcome, so that the Father receives us as He receives His beloved Son. Paul stands between the wronged master and the guilty slave and offers to bear the loss; Christ stands between a holy God and guilty sinners and bears the loss in full. Paul offers an IOU written in ink; Christ signs our pardon in His own blood. The shadow here helps us feel the weight of the reality.
study.god: God is revealed as the reconciler who does not merely excuse wrong but absorbs its cost. In Paul's readiness to pay a debt he never ran up, we catch the very heart of God, who in Christ takes responsibility for what we owe so that we, the guilty and the estranged, can be brought home and welcomed as family. This is grace that is neither cheap nor pretended, but paid for.

crossReferences.note: God made Christ, who knew no sin, to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God in Him, the exchange Paul pictures here.
God cancelled the record of debt against us, nailing it to the cross, the reality behind charge it to my account.
God shows His love in that while we were still sinners Christ died for us, the costly welcome of the guilty.
We are accepted in the Beloved; the Father receives us as He receives His Son, mirrored in receive him as you would receive me.

study.oneStory: The whole Bible moves toward a Mediator who pays what we owe and secures our welcome with the Father. Paul's charge it to my account is one small, bright reflection of that gospel: reconciliation is real, it is costly, and it rests on a substitute who takes our place. In this little letter the grand story of the cross is rehearsed in the affairs of a slave, a master, and an apostle in chains.

sermon.title: Refresh My Heart
sermon.why: The closing message of Begg's series A Study in Philemon covers Philemon 1:17-25, including this passage, and draws out how Paul's offer to bear the debt pictures the gospel and how the appeal is made in love. A faithful, orthodox handling of this movement.

quiz.q: What does Paul ask Philemon to do with Onesimus in verse 17?

options.text: Receive him exactly as he would receive Paul himself.
options.note: On the basis of their partnership, Paul asks that Onesimus be welcomed with Paul's own welcome.

options.text: Welcome him back warmly as a fellow believer.
options.note: Right direction, but Paul asks for more: receive him as he would receive Paul himself.

options.text: Give him a fair hearing before deciding.
options.note: Paul asks for full welcome as a brother, not a hearing.

options.text: Send him back to Paul at once.
options.note: Paul has sent Onesimus to Philemon; here he asks that he be received.

quiz.q: What does Paul offer to do about any debt or wrong Onesimus owes?

options.text: Charge it to Paul's own account; he will repay it himself.
options.note: Paul takes the debt on himself and signs the promise in his own hand, verses 18 to 19.

options.text: Remind Philemon the debt is trivial, since Philemon owes Paul his very self.
options.note: Paul does say this, yet his explicit offer is to personally repay whatever is owed.

options.text: Ask the church to cover the loss together.
options.note: Paul personally assumes the debt; he does not pass it to the church.

options.text: Argue that Onesimus owes nothing at all.
options.note: Paul allows the debt may be real and offers to cover it, rather than denying it.

quiz.q: How does this passage picture the gospel?

options.text: Paul stands in for the guilty, taking the debt and asking that Onesimus be received in his place, just as Christ takes our sin and gives us His welcome.
options.note: Charge it to my account and receive him as me picture imputation and substitution, 2 Corinthians 5:21.

options.text: It shows that debts should always be forgiven and forgotten.
options.note: It teaches costly reconciliation, but the deeper picture is Christ bearing our debt in our place.

options.text: It teaches that we must earn our own acceptance with God.
options.note: The opposite: acceptance comes because another pays the debt for us.

options.text: It has no connection to the gospel at all.
options.note: This is the letter's clearest gospel picture.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by asking Philemon to refresh his heart?

options.text: He asks Philemon to do to him the very kindness he praised Philemon for, by welcoming Onesimus.
options.note: Verse 20 echoes verse 7; welcoming Onesimus would refresh Paul's heart, using splagchna and anapauo.

options.text: He longs to be encouraged and cheered by Philemon's obedience.
options.note: That obedience would encourage him, but specifically the glad welcome of Onesimus is what would refresh his heart.

options.text: He is asking Philemon to send him money.
options.note: The request is for the refreshment of a welcomed brother, not funds.

options.text: He is complaining that no one cares for him.
options.note: Paul writes in warm confidence, not complaint.

quiz.q: Why does Paul mention preparing a guest room and his hope to visit?

options.text: He hopes to be released and to come, which gently encourages Philemon to do the right thing.
options.note: The coming visit adds warm accountability to the appeal, verse 22.

options.text: He genuinely longs to see Philemon and the church again.
options.note: He does long to visit, and the hoped-for visit also lends gentle accountability to the appeal.

options.text: He is demanding free lodging as his right.
options.note: It is a hopeful request among friends, not a demand of right.

options.text: He has already been released and is on his way.
options.note: Paul writes as a prisoner hoping to be restored to them, not yet freed.

questions: Paul offered to pay a debt he never ran up so that another could be welcomed. Where might God be calling you to absorb a cost so someone else can be restored?
Charge it to my account is a picture of what Christ did for you. How does it move you to know that your debt was charged to Him and His welcome given to you?
Paul asked that Onesimus be received as if he were Paul himself. Who could you welcome, forgive, or advocate for as though you were welcoming Christ?
Paul was confident Philemon would do even more than asked. How does being believed in and expected to do good shape the way you respond to others' trust?
Real reconciliation cost Paul something. Is there a relationship where you have been waiting for it to be free, when love may be asking you to pay?

translationNotes.note: Charge it to my account renders ellogeo, a bookkeeping term for reckoning a debt to someone; the same accounting idea underlies the New Testament's language of imputation.
The BSB prints an em dash after I will repay it; it has been rendered as a semicolon here per house style. I, Paul, write this with my own hand marks a formal, personally guaranteed promise.
Let me have some benefit renders the Greek onaimen, a play on the name Onesimus, which means useful or beneficial, per the BSB footnote.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Philemon 1:17-22. The em dash after I will repay it in verse 19 has been rendered as a semicolon per house style.
